---
title: "4.9 ArrayList Traversals"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## ArrayList Traversals

### Array traversal ideas carry over, but list access uses methods

The textbook keeps the bridge explicit:

::: {.tight-list}
- `ArrayList` can be traversed with enhanced `for`, indexed `for`, or `while`
- indices still start at `0`
- element access uses methods such as `get(index)`
:::

The main shift is that the collection size can change during runtime.

## Enhanced For Loop

### Use for-each when reading all values without changing list size

The retained rule is simple:

::: {.tight-list}
- enhanced `for` works when students only need the values
- it is concise and avoids index bookkeeping
- it should <span class="mark">not</span> be used when the list size changes during traversal
:::

Adding or removing elements during an enhanced `for` traversal can cause a <span class="term">ConcurrentModificationException</span>.

## Indexed For Loops and Bounds

### Use indexed access when position matters

```java
for (int i = 0; i < list.size(); i++)
{
    System.out.println(list.get(i));
}
```

Key retained ideas:

::: {.tight-list}
- `ArrayList` indices run from `0` to `size() - 1`
- access uses `get(index)` rather than `[]`
- invalid indices cause <span class="term">IndexOutOfBoundsException</span>
:::

## While Loops and Removal

### Size-changing traversals need careful index control

The textbook keeps a specific removal warning:

::: {.tight-list}
- removing an element shifts later elements left
- if the index is always incremented, the next shifted element may be skipped
- after a remove, the index often needs to stay in place for the next pass
:::

This is one of the core tracing patterns students must learn for mutable lists.

## Reading Files with `java.nio.file`

### This is enrichment, but it clarifies why lists are useful

The retained teaching idea is:

::: {.tight-list}
- `readAllLines` can return a `List<String>`
- dynamic list storage avoids knowing the final size in advance
- this reinforces why `ArrayList` is useful for real input data
:::

This part is not the AP core, but it supports the conceptual transition from files to dynamic collections.

## ArrayList of Objects

### Traversal works for object lists as well as primitive-wrapper lists

Students should be able to:

::: {.tight-list}
- traverse a list of objects
- call methods on each object in the list
- connect this back to earlier `Student` and `WordPair` class work
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

![Word pair construction pattern](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-9-arraylist-traversals/wordpairs.png){fig-align="center" width="54%"}

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- compare enhanced `for`, indexed `for`, and `while` traversals
- identify when list-size changes break enhanced `for`
- trace removals without skipping shifted elements
- read and traverse lists of objects
- <span class="term">4.9.6 Coding Challenge: FRQ Word Pairs</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain how `ArrayList` traversal differs from array traversal
- identify when an enhanced `for` loop is appropriate
- explain why removing items during traversal can skip elements
- state the valid index range using `size()`
- explain how object lists are traversed and used
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
